confirmed.title: Missing tokens can authenticate a session
confirmed.detail: At lines 10-17, verifySession passes the provided token and stored token to tokensMatch, which uses loose equality. If createSession is called without a token, stored.token is undefined; a request with providedToken undefined then returns true. I verified this with createSession('u1') followed by verifySession(id, undefined), which printed true.
confirmed.verifierReason: I could not refute it. In src/auth.js, createSession(userId, token) stores { userId, token } even when token is omitted, so stored.token is undefined. verifySession only checks that the session id exists, then returns tokensMatch(providedToken, stored.token). tokensMatch uses loose equality, and undefined == undefined is true, so verifySession(id, undefined) or verifySession(id) authenticates a known session created without a token. Scope: attacker still needs a valid sessionId, but the missing-token auth bypass itself is real.

confirmed.title: Client-controlled request body grants arbitrary roles
confirmed.detail: At lines 22-24, authorize derives role directly from req.body.role and returns allowed: true with that role. A caller can send { body: { role: 'admin' } } and receive { allowed: true, role: 'admin' }, allowing privilege escalation without any server-side authorization source.
confirmed.verifierReason: Verified directly in src/auth.js: authorize(req) sets const role = req.body.role || 'user' and returns { allowed: true, role } with no server-side lookup, validation, or caller-side mitigation in this repo. A runtime check of authorize({ body: { role: 'admin' } }) returned {"allowed":true,"role":"admin"}. The finding is real.

confirmed.title: Auth bypass when session tokens are missing or loosely equal
confirmed.detail: `tokensMatch()` uses loose equality, and `createSession()` accepts `token` without validation. A caller can create a session with `createSession(userId)` or `createSession(userId, undefined)`, then `verifySession(sessionId, undefined)` returns true because `undefined == undefined`. The same comparison also accepts coerced values such as `0` and `'0'`, so non-identical token inputs can authenticate.
confirmed.verifierReason: Confirmed from src/auth.js:10-17 and 27-30. tokensMatch(provided, stored) returns provided == stored, and createSession(userId, token) stores token without validation. Calling createSession(userId) stores token as undefined; verifySession(sessionId, undefined) then returns true because tokensMatch(undefined, undefined) is true. Loose equality also allows coercions like 0 == '0'. The only limiting condition is that the sessionId must refer to an existing session.

confirmed.title: Client-controlled role grants privilege escalation
confirmed.detail: `authorize()` reads `req.body.role` directly and returns it as the authorized role while always setting `allowed: true`. A request body like `{ "role": "admin" }` is therefore enough to receive `{ allowed: true, role: 'admin' }`, with no server-side lookup or allowlist validation.
confirmed.verifierReason: Verified in src/auth.js. authorize(req) is declared at line 22; line 23 reads req.body.role directly with only a default to 'user', and line 24 returns { allowed: true, role }. I tried to refute by searching for callers or server-side validation/allowlisting, but the repo only contains this module and the planted-bugs note. A direct call like authorize({ body: { role: 'admin' } }) would return allowed true with role 'admin'.

confirmed.title: Malformed request object can crash authorization
confirmed.detail: `authorize()` dereferences `req.body.role` without checking that `req` and `req.body` exist. Calling `authorize({})` or `authorize(undefined)` throws a TypeError, so malformed or unparsed requests can fail closed only by crashing the auth path instead of returning a controlled denial.
confirmed.verifierReason: Confirmed. src/auth.js:23 is `const role = req.body.role || 'user';` with no guard for `req` or `req.body`. I tried to refute via callers/contracts, but `authorize` is exported directly and there are no repo callers constraining input. Repro: `authorize(undefined)` throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'body')`; `authorize({})` throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'role')`. `authorize({body:{}})` returns `{allowed:true, role:'user'}`, so only missing/null body crashes.

confirmed.title: Missing token can authenticate a session created without a token
confirmed.detail: Lines 10-17 and 27-29 allow `createSession(userId)` to store `{ token: undefined }`, then `verifySession(sessionId)` calls `tokensMatch(undefined, undefined)` and returns true because `==` treats them as equal. A caller that omits both tokens is authenticated instead of rejected.
confirmed.verifierReason: The finding is confirmed. In src/auth.js, createSession(userId) stores { userId, token } with token undefined when omitted; verifySession(sessionId) also passes undefined when providedToken is omitted; tokensMatch(provided, stored) returns provided == stored, so undefined == undefined is true. A runtime check returned {"verifyWithoutToken":true,"verifyWithUndefined":true,"verifyWithWrong":false}, so an omitted token authenticates for a session created without a token.

confirmed.title: Client-supplied role is trusted for authorization
confirmed.detail: Lines 22-24 set `role` directly from `req.body.role` and always return `allowed: true`. A request body containing `{ "role": "admin" }` is accepted as admin without checking server-side session/user state, so any caller can escalate privileges.
confirmed.verifierReason: The finding is substantiated by src/auth.js itself. authorize(req) sets role from req.body.role || 'user' and returns { allowed: true, role } with no session lookup, user lookup, token check, or server-side role source. A repo-wide search found no call site or wrapper that sanitizes or overwrites req.body.role before authorize is used. A body like {"role":"admin"} would therefore be reflected as role: "admin" by the authorization result.

confirmed.title: Malformed request object crashes authorization instead of denying
confirmed.detail: Line 23 dereferences `req.body.role` without checking that `req` and `req.body` exist. Calling `authorize({})`, `authorize(null)`, or using this before body parsing throws a TypeError rather than returning a controlled denial/error, turning malformed requests into a server error path.
confirmed.verifierReason: Verified against src/auth.js and runtime behavior. authorize(req) executes `const role = req.body.role || 'user';` with no guard for `req` or `req.body`. Calling `authorize({})`, `authorize(null)`, or `authorize({ body: null })` throws TypeError before returning any denial. `authorize({ body: {} })` does not throw, but malformed/missing request objects do, so the finding is real.

result.report:
## Review Report

### `src/auth.js`

#### Missing tokens can authenticate a session

`verifySession()` authenticates a session when both the provided token and stored token are missing. `createSession(userId)` stores `token` as `undefined`, and `verifySession(sessionId)` passes `undefined` into `tokensMatch()`. Because `tokensMatch()` uses loose equality, `undefined == undefined` returns `true`.

Evidence: `createSession('u1')` followed by `verifySession(id, undefined)` returned `true`. Loose equality also allows coerced token matches such as `0 == '0'`.

#### Client-controlled request body grants arbitrary roles

`authorize()` reads `req.body.role` directly and returns it as the authorized role while always setting `allowed: true`. A caller can send `{ body: { role: 'admin' } }` and receive `{ allowed: true, role: 'admin' }`.

Evidence: `authorize({ body: { role: 'admin' } })` returned `{"allowed":true,"role":"admin"}`. There is no server-side session lookup, user lookup, role allowlist, or validation in the module.

#### Malformed request object crashes authorization

`authorize()` dereferences `req.body.role` without checking that `req` and `req.body` exist. Malformed requests throw a `TypeError` instead of returning a controlled denial or error.

Evidence: `authorize(undefined)`, `authorize({})`, `authorize(null)`, and `authorize({ body: null })` throw before returning. `authorize({ body: {} })` returns `{ allowed: true, role: 'user' }`, confirming the crash path is specifically missing or null request/body input.
